text:
Running Code
When you click the Render button a document will be generated that includes both content and the output of embedded code. You can embed code like this:

1 + 1

[1] 2


You can add options to executable code like this


[1] 4


The echo: false option disables the printing of code (only output is displayed).

title: Final Project Assignment 1
text:
Final Project Research Question and Dataset
Neil Bennett | PSCI - 3200 | Sunday, February 22, 2026
When countries face fiscal distress, they often turn to the IMF for emergency loans. These loans almost always come with conditions, requiring governments to cut public spending, reduce subsidies, privatize state-owned enterprises, or liberalize trade. The political economy logic posits that these reforms restore macroeconomic stability, but critics argue that the burden of adjustment falls disproportionately on the poor and working class as cuts to public spending often hit lower-income households hardest. The existing literature is rather contested, creating an interesting area for further investigation. Therefore my proposed research question is: Do countries that enter IMF loan programs experience larger increases in income inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient, compared to similar countries that do not?
To answer this, I am hoping to use a two-way fixed effects regression on a panel of low- and middle-income countries. This will allow me to compare each country’s Gini coefficient in years when it was under an IMF program against its own Gini in years when it was not, while accounting for global trends affecting all countries simultaneously. This isolates the within-country effect of program participation rather than simply comparing richer and poorer countries to each other. To ensure the result isn’t driven by other factors that move alongside IMF program, I will control for GDP per capita, GDP growth, government expenditure, and unemployment. I will also likely need to narrow the scope of this analysis, so I am tentatively going to focus on Sub-Saharan Africa from 1990-2015.
I will source my IMF loan data from Dr. Axel Dreher’s IMF Programs and World Bank Projects, 1970-2019 dataset, which provides yearly dummies for the existence of IMF programs in 160 countries. This dataset delineates between eight types of IMF programs, with seperate pages indicating whether each was merely signed, in effect for five months, or augmented. Since I am only interested in whether the program was actually put in place (rather than just signed or later expanded), I will focus on the “five month dummies.” Out of the eight program types, I am interested in the following: Standby Arrangements, Extended Fund Facility, Structural Adjustment Facility, and Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility. I will discard the other four as they are precautionary credit lines that generally do not carry the conditions I am interested in.
To measure income inequality, I rely on gini coefficients sourced from the Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality’s “All the Ginis Dataset.” This covers the period 1948-2017 and includes 5,121 Ginis from 201 countries and territories.
Milanovic, Branko. “All the Ginis (ALG) Dataset.” Version February 2019. Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality, CUNY Graduate Center. https://stonecenter.gc.cuny.edu/publications/all-the-ginis-alg-dataset-version-february-2019/.
Dreher, Axel, 2006, IMF and Economic Growth: The Effects of Programs, Loans, and Compliance with Conditionality, World Development 34, 5: 769-788.

title: Final Project Assignment 1
text:
Final Project Research Question and Dataset
Neil Bennett | PSCI - 3200 | Sunday, February 22, 2026
When countries face fiscal distress, they often turn to the IMF for emergency loans. These loans almost always come with conditions, requiring governments to cut public spending, reduce subsidies, privatize state-owned enterprises, or liberalize trade. The political economy logic posits that these reforms restore macroeconomic stability, but critics argue that the burden of adjustment falls disproportionately on the poor and working class as cuts to public spending often hit lower-income households hardest. The existing literature is rather contested, creating an interesting area for further investigation. Therefore my proposed research question is: Do countries that enter IMF loan programs experience larger increases in income inequality, as measured by the Gini coefficient, compared to similar countries that do not?
To answer this, I am hoping to use a two-way fixed effects regression on a panel of low- and middle-income countries. This will allow me to compare each country’s Gini coefficient in years when it was under an IMF program against its own Gini in years when it was not, while accounting for global trends affecting all countries simultaneously. This isolates the within-country effect of program participation rather than simply comparing richer and poorer countries to each other. To ensure the result isn’t driven by other factors that move alongside IMF program, I will control for GDP per capita, GDP growth, government expenditure, and unemployment. I will also likely need to narrow the scope of this analysis, so I am tentatively going to focus on Sub-Saharan Africa from 1990-2015.
I will source my IMF loan data from Dr. Axel Dreher’s IMF Programs and World Bank Projects, 1970-2019 dataset, which provides yearly dummies for the existence of IMF programs in 160 countries. This dataset delineates between eight types of IMF programs, with seperate pages indicating whether each was merely signed, in effect for five months, or augmented. Since I am only interested in whether the program was actually put in place (rather than just signed or later expanded), I will focus on the “five month dummies.” Out of the eight program types, I am interested in the following: Standby Arrangements, Extended Fund Facility, Structural Adjustment Facility, and Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility. I will discard the other four as they are precautionary credit lines that generally do not carry the conditions I am interested in.
To measure income inequality, I rely on gini coefficients sourced from the Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality’s “All the Ginis Dataset.” This covers the period 1948-2017 and includes 5,121 Ginis from 201 countries and territories.
Milanovic, Branko. “All the Ginis (ALG) Dataset.” Version February 2019. Stone Center on Socio-Economic Inequality, CUNY Graduate Center. https://stonecenter.gc.cuny.edu/publications/all-the-ginis-alg-dataset-version-february-2019/.
Dreher, Axel, 2006, IMF and Economic Growth: The Effects of Programs, Loans, and Compliance with Conditionality, World Development 34, 5: 769-788.
